title: Good Job, Good Benefits, Leadership Questions
body: Good Job, Good Benefits, Leadership Questions
pros: Good pay, good benefits, great work/life balance and flexibility, really good PTO. Coworkers are friendly and helpful and first couple levels of management are very understanding and competent.
cons: Been lots of leadership turnover and what's felt like 5 or 6 internal reorganizations. Leadership doesn't inspire tons of confidence and people can be concerned about layoffs. Raises are variable, some years goods, some years not. Structure and opportunities for advancement are vague.

title: Pros and Cons of Working at Nokia
body: Pros and Cons of Working at Nokia
pros: Nokia has a very mature technical and process framework. The teams are well-organized, and the company has deep expertise in cloud-native and telecom-grade products, which provides great opportunities for systematic technical growth.
cons: As a large organization, decision-making and resource coordination can sometimes be slow, so adopting new tools or technologies often requires extra communication and longer lead time.

title: Mediocre "on-the-job experience"
pros: The pay is decent and working in a hybrid environment is okay. They provide decent benefits as well.
cons: Youre basically a tier I ticket clearing peon, so you dont really learn anything useful. They middle management will use you and abuse you till you are worn out. They will also dangle a carrot in front of our face with the prospect of employment after you long-term contract ends. However, every person I've known that's worked there has ended up getting ghosted by them. That's not that bad and all, but if you're unfortunate as I am, they end ruining potential employment opportunities for you in the future even though they said you can use them as a reference and put countless hours of OT in for them anytime they needed coverage.

body: Flexible Work Arrangements and Nice PTO, but Lack of Career Development and Growth
pros: 1) Generous Vacation Time, even when starting new. 2) Flexible Work Arrangements. 3) Work-from-home arrangements are available, depending on your position and tasks.
cons: 1) Lack of career growth, career development, and career mentorship opportunities. This can be very frustrating for early-career engineers (0 - 5 year range). For such a large company, Nokia needs to do a better job of retaining and developing its younger engineers. 2) Very slow processes and work pace. Excessive bureaucracy. This is partially due to the heavy dependency on the Finland Nokia office. A lot of technical information that's needed is in the Finland office. This can be quite time-consuming and frustrating at times, and cause unnecessary delays, due to the 8-hour time difference between Dallas, TX and Finland. This may be discouraging for engineers who are trying to build their careers and gain valuable work experience. 3) Unfortunately, there is no 401k match provided with the benefits. 4) Vacation time cannot be carried over to the next calendar year. It is a use-it-or-lose-it program. At times, this can force employees to take "unwanted vacations" just to not lose their PTO time.

title: Good Place to Work
pros: Cutting edge technology. Work with seasoned experts in the field. Pension is great. Company wants you to make your career here and stay for a long time. Long time employees have a great work ethic and deep knowledge of the products as well as the nitty gritty details. Pay is good. Steady job, great job security (despite occasional global layoffs). Overall, quality of the hardware engineers here is great; excellent attention to detail. People are very nice and kind, in a genuine way. It is nice to work with genuine good hearted people, and you will find many here. You are building products which are the backbone of the Internet communications infrastructure. Management is highly technically adept, there is little "fat" in the organization as far as I could tell.
cons: Not much collaboration between team members, much of the work is individually focused. While many people indeed contribute to the creation of each product, the team dynamic is non-existent because of the manner in which the work is doled out individually. Little investment into cultivating employees, lack of programs to adequately train newer employees. Average age of employees is quite high, which results in a subpar culture for young engineers. Little recognition for work performed (perhaps the managers are overburdened with so much work that they cannot spare the time to celebrate any achievements. But this results in degraded performance in the long term, as there are few incentives to perform other than money. They should realize that employees will do their best work when achievements are recognized). Morale seems low, unfortunately.

cons: As an international company, having several offices worldwide, a considerable amount of time is spent on planning, which has a downside and impact on career growth. At Nokia, there is a high chance that you learn about internal tools which are not used in the job market. Nokia is not amongst the companies that offer the highest salaries.

title: Decent wlb, good benefits, a bit outdated in management
body: Decent wlb, good benefits, a bit outdated in management
pros: - Many truly brilliant individual contributors who are technically competent and probably were around when the basic kernels of modern technology were being developed. - People really do love technology at this company. - Depending on your team, you can have decent freedom to explore interesting avenues for ideas and directions. - Seems like the company might be turning around with the latest batch of global leaders.
cons: - People with actual skill building and developing ideas are often used as the workhorses for people who are good at developing "paper architectures" and ideas. - TC is not competitive, and career growth is slow/non-existent due to saturation at the top. - Constant turnover in leadership, which has led to constant re-organization and layoffs. - Constant internal messaging about how the company is changing moral X or core value Y, which cheapens what the leadership is trying to convey. - Ideas in research and development often never get validated by possible customers in target markets to find product market fit.

title: Telecom industry is not a sunrise industry, but won't be sunset
body: Telecom industry is not a sunrise industry, but won't be sunset
pros: 1. Great engineer company, unlike some small companies where the managers dominate. In Nokia, the engineers are the key values. 2. Many senior employees are at retired age, there is more opportunities for young engineers actually. 3. Good benefits, not that much push from management 4. With 5G coming out, Nokia has a chance to be back on the stage. If you join now, maybe there is something you can expect in the near future
cons: 1. If you like the city life, it might not be a good idea to work in Nokia. 2. Many colleagues are senior engineers, not just their levels, I mean the age. No offense, you can learn a lot from them about telecom knowledge, but it means there won't be that many company events, cause most of them are not that age. 3. Lunch is boring

title: Acquisition Impact
pros: Bell Labs and the R&D organizations of former Alcatel-Lucent, Lucent Technologies and AT&T were great places to work before the recent acquisition by Nokia. The technical assignments were challenging and cutting edge. The environment encouraged innovation and sharing ideas.
cons: The environment has changed to a narrow technological vision formulated in Finland. Our teams aren't empowered and no meaningful leadership is recognized outside geographic boundaries. Most important R&D activities were relocated or are in the process of migration to Eastern Europe and Asia. The product development, profitability and ultimately customer satisfaction have suffered from recent corporate strategies. If you are looking for a rewarding engineering career in North America, this isn't the right place.
role: Current employee - Member Of Technical Staff

title: Once a great nordic success story now an Asian IT service company
body: Once a great nordic success story now an Asian IT service company
pros: Good benefits Healthy work life balance Ability to coast along doing nothing
cons: This was once a great innovative Finnish company. After 10yrs with Rajeev Suri at the helm it has become an Indo-Finnish service corporation too big to change and innovate, instead it focuses all its energy on cutting costs and outsourcing work to India. Nokia was quite a diverse company but now all management positions are occupied by Indians, When I left I was the only non-Indian on my team. Despite many the many cost cutting layoffs, Nokia never dismisses any senior managers, directors or vice presidents. These well paid do-nothing oligarchs simply rotate job roles every 6months to a year. Also, despite the NAM region being their biggest and most profitable region, all decisions are made by Global, probably at the spa of Karaportii

title: It depends....
pros: Nokia is a HUGE company, so it is critical to understand where you're going (will you be a part of Business Unit, Regional staff, Admin function that reports to Finland, etc.). Your experience will vary a Lot depending on where you work. I'm only familiar with a part of Nokia that develops networking equipment. The culture of this group is very different from the rest of the company. The group originally was formed by f-Alcatel purchase of a Silicon Valley start-up, so many people has done one+ start-ups and consequently the culture reflects it.
cons: It's important to read Pros. Cons - you are a part of a very large company. If you're a superstar, it might not hurt you, but if you're "just a great above expectations employee", you might face with the fact that in order to increase xxx (whatever you want to increase), you'll have to look elsewhere. The pros - your paycheck comes regularly. The cons - it is hard to get anything special related to compensation because if your business/your role is doing great, there is certainly another business within such large company that is doing poorly. No stock (technically there is stock, but ... $5-stock with no stock grants is the same as "no stock)

title: Loved it
pros: Very employee friendly, I had a great supportive boss, this is a truly global company and I was lucky to travel and visit many office locations all over the world. My coworkers were really nice. The company generously invests in training. Nokia reflects the Finnish way of life where work life balance is very strongly respected, employee benefits are solid including a 401 k match and generous PTO. I loved the work and being at Nokia and the company consistently lived up to its values. I left Nokia after a round of layoffs but I would return if the opportunity presents again.
cons: Cash comp was on the lower side and equity was not impressive but there were several other perks (like PTO, 401k match, work life balance) that sort of made up some of the shortcomings. Overall Nokia is still finding its feet after the collapse of its mobile phone business. The mobile networks business while sizable is too cyclical and low margin mostly due to overwhelming opex costs. I also feel Nokia for its revenue and market cap size has a significantly large (>100k) employee base which will continue to hinder the companyâs resurgence as a solid and nimble competitor to Chinese and American counterparts. The management is quite unsure and fickle minded about the business beyond mobile networks like software and consumer. So there is a lot of âone step forward and two steps backâ initiatives that go around like the Ozo, Digital Health etc.
